step.title: Step 17
step.decisionPreview: print("Checking if game is truly over or if we can continue...")

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 32, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 31 of about 39 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 31 actions returned the board to a state already seen 31 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 31 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
GAME_OVER! Moving the blue block too far right caused game over. The blue block hit the right wall. So there's a right boundary that causes game over when crossed.
This means I need to be more careful about where I move the blue block. Let me check the current state and figure out the boundaries.
The blue block was at cols 20-39, then moved RIGHT 2 more times (8 cols), putting it at cols 28-47. But then game_over happened. So the right boundary is somewhere around col 47-48.
Wait, but the board is 64 cols wide. So the blue block can't go all the way to the right edge. There must be a boundary at some column.
Let me check the current blue block position and understand the boundaries better.
Also, the game is over now. I need to figure out how to restart or continue. Let me check if there's a way to restart the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over - check current state
curr_seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
blue = [n for n in curr_seg if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"Blue blocks: {len(blue)}")
if blue:
    print(f"Blue: {blue[0]['boundary']}")
print(f"Score: {last_action_result.get('score')}")
print(f"State: {last_action_result.get('state')}")
# Check all nodes to see what's on the board now
for n in curr_seg:
    if n['color'] not in ['O', 'w', 'g', 'G', 'c']:  # Skip background colors
        print(f"{n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After game over, the blue block is back at its original position: rows 16-19, cols 12-31. The charcoal block is gone (it was at rows 1-3, cols 36-39). The magenta block is still at rows 4-7, cols 36-39. The yellow U-shapes are still there. The green line at row 0 is now light green (N instead of some other color).
So the game over state resets the blue block to its original position. The charcoal block disappeared. This is important information.
Wait, looking more carefully:
- The charcoal block (c) is NOT in the current segmentation. It was there initially at rows 1-3, cols 36-39.
- The magenta block (M) is still at rows 4-7, cols 36-39.
- The blue block (b) is back at rows 16-19, cols 12-31 (original position).
- The yellow U-shapes (Y) are still at the bottom.
- The top line is now light green (N) at row 0.
So the game over happened because I moved the blue block too far right. The charcoal block disappeared. Maybe the charcoal block was a "danger zone" or "obstacle" that I shouldn't have moved through?
Wait, but the blue block was at cols 20-39 when I moved RIGHT twice more. The blue block would have been at cols 28-47 after those moves. The charcoal block was at cols 36-39. So the blue block would have overlapped with the charcoal block's column range. Maybe that's what caused the game over?
Actually, looking at the initial state:
- Charcoal: rows 1-3, cols 36-39
- Magenta: rows 4-7, cols 36-39
- Blue: rows 16-19, cols 12-31
The charcoal and magenta blocks are stacked vertically at cols 36-39. The blue block starts at cols 12-31, which doesn't overlap with cols 36-39.
When I moved the blue block RIGHT, it eventually reached cols 20-39, which overlaps with cols 36-39. Then I moved RIGHT 2 more times, which would put the blue block at cols 28-47, which definitely overlaps with cols 36-39.
So maybe the game over happened because the blue block overlapped with the magenta/charcoal column range? Or maybe the game over happened because the blue block went too far right (past some boundary)?
Let me think about this differently. The game over happened after moving RIGHT 2 times from cols 20-39. The blue block would be at cols 28-47. But the board is 64 cols wide, so cols 28-47 is still within the board. So the game over wasn't because the blue block went off the board.
Maybe the game over happened because the blue block touched the right wall of some invisible boundary? Or maybe the game over happened because the blue block overlapped with the magenta block's column range?
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the result. The action was stopped early with `stop_reason='game_over'`. Only 2 out of 6 RIGHT actions were executed. So the game over happened after the 2nd RIGHT action, which moved the blue block from cols 20-39 to cols 28-47.
Hmm, but the blue block is now back at its original position (cols 12-31). So the game over reset the blue block. The charcoal block is gone. This suggests that the game over was caused by something specific.
Let me try to restart the game and be more careful. Maybe I need to move the blue block to a specific position without going too far right.
Actually, I notice the charcoal block is gone. Maybe the charcoal block was a "life" or "health" indicator, and when I caused game over, it disappeared. Or maybe the charcoal block was a "target" that I needed to avoid.
Let me try to understand the game better. The valid actions are still UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE. Let me try moving the blue block again to see if the game is still playable.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over but actions still valid - let me try moving to see if the game resets
# First check if the charcoal block is really gone
print("Checking if game is truly over or if we can continue...")
# Try a LEFT move to see if the blue block moves
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content: Checking if game is truly over or if we can continue...
